items.question: What is inertia in k-means, and why can it not select k by itself?
items.answer: Inertia (WCSS) is the sum of squared distances from each point to its assigned centroid. It decreases monotonically as k increases, reaching zero at k = n, so the minimum is trivial—use the elbow bend or complementary metrics instead.

items.question: Explain the silhouette coefficient and its range.
items.answer: For each point, s(i) = (b(i) − a(i)) / max(a(i), b(i)) where a(i) is mean intra-cluster distance and b(i) is mean distance to the nearest other cluster. Values near +1 indicate well-separated membership; near 0 means on a boundary; negative suggests misassignment. The overall score is the mean of s(i) in [-1, 1].

items.question: When would you report ARI vs silhouette?
items.answer: Report silhouette (internal) when no ground-truth labels exist—it judges cohesion and separation in feature space. Report ARI (external) when reference labels exist to quantify chance-corrected agreement between clusters and truth. They answer different questions and are not interchangeable.

items.question: What is homogeneity, and how does it differ from completeness?
items.answer: Homogeneity measures whether each cluster contains only members of a single true class (pure clusters). Completeness measures whether all members of a true class land in one cluster (classes fully grouped). A partition can be homogeneous but incomplete if a class is split across clusters.
